topic: The Archaeological Discovery of King Tut's Tomb
title: Exploring the Meticulous Methods Behind the Excavation of King Tut's Tomb

lesson.learn: On November 4, 1922, British archaeologist Howard Carter's team finally uncovered the sealed tomb of Pharaoh Tutankhamun (1330 BCE - 1323 BCE) in the Valley of the Kings, a vast necropolis spanning over 4 kilometers along the west bank of the Nile River. Carter's meticulous excavation approach, facilitated by his expertise as Chief Inspector of Antiquities in Egypt since 1907, involved careful clearance of the 1,200 tons of debris that obstructed the tomb's entrance. To accomplish this feat, his team of 20 men employed innovative techniques such as systematic recording and precision measurements to chart the narrow corridors within the tomb. This meticulous attention to detail was rewarded when the team discovered the tomb's entrance after nine years of exhaustive searching, yielding an astonishing array of artifacts, including an intricately crafted golden funerary mask, ornate furniture, and exquisite jewelry, estimated to be worth approximately $100 million in 1922.
lesson.simpler: Howard Carter's team applied cutting-edge excavation methods to uncover the entrance to King Tut's tomb, using advanced techniques such as systematic recording and precision measurements. This meticulous approach allowed them to retrieve over 5,000 artifacts that shed new light on the history of ancient Egypt.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The success of Carter's excavation underscores the importance of meticulous planning, precision measurements, and the careful analysis of multiple lines of evidence. By integrating diverse perspectives and techniques, archaeologists can gain a nuanced understanding of ancient cultures and shed light on the complexities of human societies.
lesson.deeperDive: The Valley of the Kings is renowned for its 63 tombs belonging to pharaohs and nobles, including Carter's team's discovery of King Tut's tomb. Carter's extensive search, spanning several months, was facilitated by his collaboration with French archaeologist Jean-Philippe Lauer and the use of cutting-edge techniques such as systematic recording. Interestingly, the excavation process required Carter's team to clear over 1,200 tons of debris, a formidable undertaking that demonstrates their unwavering dedication to their work.
lesson.funFact: Carter's team employed innovative tunneling techniques to reach the hidden entrance to King Tut's tomb, which was finally located using a combination of precise measurements and targeted excavations.

people: Howard Carter (British archaeologist who discovered King Tut's tomb)
Lord Carnarvon (financier and sponsor of Carter's excavations in Egypt)
Tutankhamun (Pharaoh of ancient Egypt whose tomb was discovered by Carter)
Jean-Philippe Lauer (French archaeologist who worked with Carter on the discovery)
Edward R. Ayrton (British archaeologist who contributed to the excavations in the Valley of the Kings)
places: Valley of the Kings (necropolis in Egypt where King Tut's tomb was found)
Thebes (modern-day Luxor, Egypt, which was the capital of ancient Egypt)
Cairo Museum (Egyptian museum that houses many artifacts from King Tut's tomb)
Amarna (Ancient Egyptian city where King Tut grew up)
years: 1907 (year Howard Carter was appointed as Chief Inspector of Antiquities in Egypt)
1922 (year King Tut's tomb was discovered by Carter)
1330 BCE (estimated date of King Tut's birth)
1323 BCE (estimated date of King Tut's ascension to the throne)
concepts: Archaeology (study of past human cultures through material remains and artifacts)
Egyptology (study of ancient Egyptian language, culture, history, and society)
Funerary practices (ancient customs related to death and burial rituals)
Mummification (preservation of bodies in ancient Egypt)
Tombs and monuments (architectural features used to honor the dead)
